perturbation.original_text: Definitions "Agreement" means this agreement. The Network Management Outsourcing Agreement. "CPI" means an amount determined by reference to All Groups Indexes for Urban Areas being the figure relating to the weighted urban average for PNG contained in the Consumer Price Index published by the Papua New Guinea Bureau of Statistics each quarter in the Papua New Guinea Statistical Bulletin. "Help Desk" means a physical location within Datec's head office designated as an immediate contact point to provide service assistance to customers. "Material Breach" includes (but is not necessarily limited to) the occurrence of any or more of the following: (i) a breach, or series of breaches, of this agreement which causes a substantial disruption to the Services; or (ii) an accumulation of breaches which places unreasonable burdens on the BSP Personnel, or (iii) Datec commits a breach which at common law would justify termination of this agreement, or (iv) Datec commits multiple breaches of this agreement occurring within a 6 month period; or (v) Datec fails to comply with the Transition Plan. "Business Hours" means Monday to Friday 07.30 hours to 18.00 hours.
perturbation.changed_text: Definitions "Agreement" means this agreement. The Network Management Outsourcing Agreement. "CPI" means an amount determined by reference to market conditions. "Help Desk" means a virtual service to provide service assistance to customers. "Material Breach" includes (but is not necessarily limited to) a disagreement on service quality. "Business Hours" means standard working hours.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes specific definitions and replaces them with vague alternatives. The original CPI definition was tied to the PNG Bureau of Statistics, but it has been changed to 'market conditions' which creates ambiguity, especially because the CPI is being used in section 9.1 CPI Adjustments. The Help Desk was originally defined as a physical location, the modified definition is a 'virtual service', which is inconsistent with references to physical locations in sections describing support services, for example in section 2.15 Help Desk Services there is mention of a single-point-of-contact. The definition of Material Breach is changed to just a 'disagreement on service quality' which is inconsistent with section 14.1 Termination By BSP for Cause. Similarly, defining Business Hours as 'standard working hours' creates confusion, which is inconsistent with sections like 1.5 Hours of Support.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9.1 CPI Adjustments No later than 30 days after the end of each contract year the Base Fee must be increased or decreased in accordance with the increase or decrease in the CPI and such increases or decreases will be calculated by using the following formula: R2 = R1 x New CPI Old CPI Where: R2 = the Base Fee payable for the relevant 12 month period of the Term R1 = the Base Fee specified the previous year. New CPI means the last published CPI for a complete quarter ending prior to the date of commencement of the relevant 12-month period of the term. Old CPI means the last published CPI for a complete quarter ending prior to the date of this agreement. Should the CPI increase exceed more than 10% in any given year the maximum amount applied will be 10% or which ever is lower.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces several contradictions. First, changing the CPI definition from a specific index published by the Papua New Guinea Bureau of Statistics to 'market conditions' directly contradicts section 9.1, which relies on the 'New CPI' and 'Old CPI' derived from the original specific definition. The formula in 9.1 cannot function with a vague 'market conditions' value. Second, changing the 'Help Desk' definition from a physical location to a 'virtual service' contradicts the implicit assumption of a physical contact point in sections describing support services, such as section 2.15 which refers to a 'single-point-of-contact'. This incompatibility creates uncertainty about the nature of the help desk services. Finally, redefining 'Material Breach' as merely a 'disagreement on service quality' significantly weakens the original definition, which included specific, serious breaches related to service disruption and non-compliance, thereby conflicting with the grounds for termination outlined in section 14.1 Termination By BSP for Cause.

perturbation.original_text: 8.5 Rebates 1. Should Datec not achieve the agreed Service Levels contained in the Service Level Agreement as detailed in Schedule B, a monthly rebate will be applied as per the Rebate Table in Pricing Schedule C, 2. Rebates will apply to Severity Levels One (1) and Two (2) during the agreed business hours detailed in Schedule B. 3. The maximum rebate in any given month will be 50% of the monthly base fee. The rebate calculation will be completed before the 15th Day of the following month for the preceding month together with supporting monthly reports and provided to the BSP. 4. For the purposes of determining SLA achievement the "Time to Respond" measurement will be used. The following definition will apply: "Time to Respond" means, the time between the problem being logged and the customer being given a job number and the technician beginning the job" As this applies to Severity Levels One (1) and Two (2) this will also include the informing of Datec's senior operational management.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.5 Rebates Should Datec and BSP mutually agree in the moment that Datec has failed to achieve satisfactory performance, a rebate *may* be applied. The specific amount of the rebate, if any, will be determined at the sole discretion of BSP management. The rebate calculation will be at BSP's discretion. *Time to Respond* means whatever BSP considers an acceptable duration.
perturbation.explanation: This change significantly alters the rebates clause, removing objective criteria for rebate calculation and replacing it with subjective determinations by BSP. By changing the language to 'may' be applied based on mutual agreement, it contradicts the detailed SLA achievement metrics in Schedule B. The 'Time to Respond' clause is now at BSP's discretion, contradicting the specific definitions in Schedule B. This undermines the enforceability of the SLA and creates uncertainty regarding financial consequences of not meeting service levels, the language is now discretionary and inconsistent. This contradicts the original intent of objectively measurable standards.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Schedule B Datec Service Levels for BSP Networking Outsourcing Project Services to be performed are defined in the Network Management Outsourcing Agreement. These include Data services, network services and internet services. As referenced in Section 8.5 of this agreement, rebates will be applied as detailed in Pricing Schedule C, should Datec not achieve the agreed Service Levels. Rebates will apply to Severity Levels One (1) and Two (2) for all logged jobs during the agreed business hours contained in this agreement. A. Helpdesk / Service Calls 1(a) During Business Hours (Monday to Friday 7.30 to 18.00) Severity Level Time to Respond Escalation Time Comments 1 15 minutes 1 hour 2 30 minutes 2 hours 3 1 hour 4 hours 4 8 hours Next business day
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces objective criteria for rebates based on SLA achievement (detailed in Schedule B and Pricing Schedule C) with subjective determinations by BSP management. The phrase 'may be applied' and 'at BSP's discretion' directly contradicts the original text and Schedule B, which mandates rebates based on specific service level failures and defines 'Time to Respond.' This undermines the enforceability of the SLA by removing the previously defined objective standards. This directly clashes with the contradicted text, which references Section 8.5 and expects rebates to be applied according to Pricing Schedule C based on failing to meet agreed-upon service levels as detailed in Schedule B. The modification introduces uncertainty and potentially eliminates the rebate obligation, creating a strong in-text contradiction, as per Omissions - In Text Contradiction, definition.

perturbation.original_text: 14.1 Termination By BSP for Cause If so advised the BSP may terminate this agreement by giving written notice to Datec if: (a) Datec materially breaches the agreement and the breach ("Material Breach") cannot be, or is not, rectified within 30 days after a notice from the BSP, (b) an Insolvency Event occurs in relation to Datec (c) any other event specified in this agreement as giving rise to a right for the BSP to terminate immediately occurs; or (d) Datec commits a series of breaches that together constitute a Material Breach.
perturbation.changed_text: 14.1 Termination By BSP for Cause BSP may terminate this agreement at any time, without cause, by giving written notice to Datec. Datec agrees to accept such termination without recourse.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the 'for cause' requirement for termination by BSP, allowing them to terminate at any time without justification. This directly contradicts multiple other sections referencing the conditions for termination, the need to demonstrate material breach, and the associated financial penalties for termination without cause. This change effectively nullifies Datec's protections and remedies under the original contract, creating an internal contradiction that undermines Datec's rights and protections.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 14.5 Termination Fee If the BSP terminates under clause 14.3 (Termination by BSP for Convenience) the BSP will pay Datec the Termination Fee calculated in accordance with Attachment C (Pricing).
perturbation.justification: The changed text allows BSP to terminate the agreement 'at any time, without cause', while the contradicted text (14.5) specifies a 'Termination Fee' only if BSP terminates 'for Convenience' under clause 14.3. The original agreement required cause for termination (14.1) now, because BSP can terminate at any time without cause, clause 14.5 becomes contradictory as the conditions under which the Termination Fee is paid are fundamentally altered, creating a strong in-text contradiction.
